summary: The reproduction bundle sets up WordPress with My Calendar, configures mc_add_events for low-privilege users, and injects a serialized PHP gadget object via the ghost event_access_hidden POST parameter to trigger the unserialize() sink and produce an observable side effect via __destruct().
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should cause the crafted serialized object injected via event_access_hidden to be deserialized by unserialize() at line 2424, instantiating the EvoHunt_Gadget class and triggering its __destruct() method, which writes a proof marker file to /tmp/evohunt_poc_success inside the container.
actual_observed_result: The reproduction bundle was generated but not executed in this session. The Docker-based environment setup, exploit trigger, and verification scripts are included for independent reproduction.
verification_method: The verify.sh helper checks for the existence and content of /tmp/evohunt_poc_success inside the container. The marker file is written only by the EvoHunt_Gadget::__destruct() method, which fires only when the injected serialized object is successfully deserialized by unserialize(). The check distinguishes successful deserialization from a generic PHP error, startup failure, or unrelated crash.
limitations_or_blockers: The reproduction requires Docker and network access to clone the target repository and download WordPress.

The PoC uses a custom gadget class (EvoHunt_Gadget) injected via auto_prepend_file to demonstrate deserialization with an observable side effect. Real-world exploitation would use WordPress core or third-party plugin gadget chains.
The exploit requires a valid WordPress user account with mc_add_events capability. The reproduction automates user creation as part of the setup.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable commit, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
